The **Chiefs** ([Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"): *Rangatira*;[[1]](#cite_note-1) formerly known as the **Waikato Chiefs** and officially called the **Gallagher Chiefs** for sponsorship reasons) are a New Zealand professional [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") team based in [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_New_Zealand "Hamilton, New Zealand"), Waikato. The team competes in the [Super Rugby Pacific](/wiki/Super_Rugby_Pacific "Super Rugby Pacific") competition, previously known as the Super 12 and Super 14, and are one of the competition's five New Zealand teams. Their primary home ground is [FMG Stadium Waikato](/wiki/FMG_Stadium_Waikato "FMG Stadium Waikato").

The Chiefs region represents seven provincial unions: Bay of Plenty, Counties Manukau, Taranaki and Waikato, who compete in the [National Provincial Championship](/wiki/National_Provincial_Championship_(2006%E2%80%93present) "National Provincial Championship (2006–present)") (NPC), and King Country, Ngati Porou East Coast and Thames Valley, who compete in the [Heartland Championship](/wiki/Heartland_Championship "Heartland Championship").

The Chiefs field three main teams: Chiefs (men's), who compete in Super Rugby Pacific, Chiefs Manawa (women's), who compete in Sky Super Rugby Aupiki and a men's under-20s team.

The Chiefs teams play in red, black and yellow colours with jerseys in recent years having strong Māori cultural elements to tie the club to its region. Māori culture, values and traditions are also a cornerstone of the club's culture, implemented during Dave Rennie's time as head coach and continued by current head coach Clayton McMillan.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Until 2004, the Chiefs were the only New Zealand side never to have qualified for the Super 12 semi-finals. In that year the Chiefs earned their first semi-final berth, and in the end achieved fourth place (defeated 37–20 in the semi-final by the [ACT Brumbies](/wiki/Brumbies_(rugby) "Brumbies (rugby)")). They subsequently reached the 2009 final, but found themselves on the short end of a record 61–17 defeat by the [Bulls](/wiki/Bulls_(rugby) "Bulls (rugby)").

The Chiefs were rewarded with a home final after a strong [2012 season](/wiki/2012_Super_Rugby_Final "2012 Super Rugby Final"). The Chiefs defeated the [Sharks](/wiki/Sharks_(rugby_union) "Sharks (rugby union)") 37–6, winning their first title. In [2013](/wiki/2013_Super_Rugby_Final "2013 Super Rugby Final"), the Chiefs became the fourth team to record back-to-back title wins, when they defeated the Brumbies 27–22 at Waikato Stadium.

In 2021 the Chiefs Rugby Club established their women's team, formerly known as the Chiefs women, now the [Chiefs Manawa](/wiki/Chiefs_Manawa "Chiefs Manawa"). The Chiefs Manawa won the inaugural season of Sky [Super Rugby Aupiki](/wiki/Super_Rugby_Aupiki "Super Rugby Aupiki") in 2022 and were runner-up in the competition in 2023 and 2024.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiefs_(Super_Rugby)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Chiefs were founded in 1996 as the Waikato Chiefs for the inaugural Super 12 season in [1996](/wiki/1996_Super_12_season "1996 Super 12 season"). Prior to the Super 12, the Super 10 competition had been in place, which [NPC](/wiki/ITM_Cup "ITM Cup") teams took part in, including Waikato. In the first year of competition the Chiefs placed 6th in the overall standings, missing out on making the finals; winning 6 of their 11 regular season matches. The following season the Chiefs placed 11th, winning 4 games and losing 7. In [1998](/wiki/1998_Super_12_season "1998 Super 12 season") the Chiefs performed closer to the standard of their 1996 season and placed at 7th in the final standings. In [1999](/wiki/1999_Super_12_season "1999 Super 12 season") the side were able to do one better and claimed 6th position on the ladder but were still yet to make the playoffs.

In [2000](/wiki/2000_Super_12_season "2000 Super 12 season") the Chiefs won 3 of their regular season games and finished the regular season in 10th place. The following season the team equalled their best position again – finishing 6th. In [2002](/wiki/2002_Super_12_season "2002 Super 12 season") the team won 4 games and lost 7 to finish in 8th position, and the season after, [2003 Super 12 season](/wiki/2003_Super_12_season "2003 Super 12 season"), fell to a 10th-place finish. But the season after, the Chiefs won 7 regular season games and came 4th on the ladder – claiming the first semi-final spot in their history. The Chiefs lost the semi-final against the Brumbies. In [2005](/wiki/2005_Super_12_season "2005 Super 12 season") the team finished 6th. In [2006](/wiki/2006_Super_14_season "2006 Super 14 season"), the Super 12 expanded to the [Super 14](/wiki/Super_14 "Super 14"), with the addition of a new Australian and South African club. The Chiefs won 7 of their 13 games and drew once with 5 losses to come 7th. In 2009, the Chiefs made their second ever semi-final, defeating the [Hurricanes](/wiki/Hurricanes_(rugby_union) "Hurricanes (rugby union)") 14 – 10 to advance to the final for the first time. They lost the final to the Bulls by the biggest-ever margin of 61–17. In the [2010](/wiki/2010_Super_14_season "2010 Super 14 season") and [2011 seasons](/wiki/2011_Super_Rugby_season "2011 Super Rugby season"), they were unable to replicate their form of 2009, missing the playoffs in both seasons.

In [2012](/wiki/2012_Super_Rugby_season "2012 Super Rugby season"), following the disappointing results of previous years, the Chiefs underwent a significant change in personnel. This included the recruitment of new coaches, including [Dave Rennie](/wiki/Dave_Rennie "Dave Rennie") and [Wayne Smith](/wiki/Wayne_Smith_(rugby_union) "Wayne Smith (rugby union)"), and players, including [Aaron Cruden](/wiki/Aaron_Cruden "Aaron Cruden"), [Ben Tameifuna](/wiki/Ben_Tameifuna "Ben Tameifuna"), [Brodie Retallick](/wiki/Brodie_Retallick "Brodie Retallick") and [Sonny Bill Williams](/wiki/Sonny_Bill_Williams "Sonny Bill Williams"). The changes had an immediate impact as the Chiefs finished at the top of the New Zealand conference, qualifying for a home semi-final, which they won, defeating the [Crusaders](/wiki/Crusaders_(rugby_union) "Crusaders (rugby union)") 20–17. They subsequently hosted the [final](/wiki/2012_Super_Rugby_final "2012 Super Rugby final") for the first time in the teams's history, comprehensively defeating the [Sharks](/wiki/Sharks_(rugby_union) "Sharks (rugby union)") by 37 – 6, claiming their first title. They also set many club records in the 2012 season, including: most home wins, best home streak, best season winning streak, and most points and tries scored.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In 2013, the Chiefs again won the Super Rugby title and the New Zealand conference with a regular-season record of 12 wins and four losses. They also won the BNZ Cup, a new trophy established by the NZRU for the New Zealand side with the best record in intra-conference matches.

In 2016 the Chiefs played a touring [Wales](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team") side, winning the one-off encounter 40–7. In doing so they became the first New Zealand Super Rugby franchise to defeat an international team.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In 2017 the Chiefs played the [British and Irish Lions](/wiki/British_and_Irish_Lions "British and Irish Lions") side in Hamilton, losing the encounter 34–6. In the regular season, they finished 3rd in the NZ conference and 6th overall, putting them through to the playoffs. They won a thriller quarter final against the Stormers in Cape Town (17–11) but lost in the semi-finals to the Crusaders (27–13)

In 2018 the Chiefs finished 3rd in the NZ conference and 5th overall. This result put them through to the quarter-finals, in which they lost a tight battle against the Hurricanes (32–31)

The Chiefs didn't have a great start to the 2019 season, losing 4 games straight off the bat, including a loss to the Sunwolves (30–15). However, the Chiefs redeemed themselves by defeating the Bulls 56–20 and going on to win more. They ended up finishing 3rd in the NZ conference and 7th overall, which put them through to the quarter-finals. However, the Chiefs would lose to the Jaguares 21–16 in Buenos Aires

After 7 rounds in 2020, the Chiefs were 3rd in the NZ conference and 5th overall. However, the COVID-19 pandemic suspended the 2020 Super Rugby season from going ahead, for travel and health reasons. Despite this, NZRU created a domestic Super Rugby tournament called Super Rugby Aotearoa, which started in June 2020. The Chiefs played well but didn't win a single game in Super Rugby Aotearoa 2020.

The Chiefs lost their first 2 games of Super Rugby Aotearoa in 2021, and then got their first win in the competition against the Hurricanes (35–29). In the 2023 season, the Chiefs won 13 of the 14 regular season matches, going on to host the Crusaders at [FMG Stadium Waikato](/wiki/FMG_Stadium_Waikato "FMG Stadium Waikato") for their first home final since 2013. The Crusaders however remained the [Super Rugby Pacific](/wiki/Super_Rugby_Pacific "Super Rugby Pacific") champions winning 25–20.

## Franchise area and ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiefs_(Super_Rugby)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Franchise area and ownership")]

### Franchise area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiefs_(Super_Rugby)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Franchise area")]

Since 1999, the Chiefs have represented the provincial unions of [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_Rugby_Union "Bay of Plenty Rugby Union"), [Counties Manukau](/wiki/Counties_Manukau_Rugby_Union "Counties Manukau Rugby Union"), [King Country](/wiki/King_Country_Rugby_Football_Union "King Country Rugby Football Union"), [Thames Valley](/wiki/Thames_Valley_Rugby_Football_Union "Thames Valley Rugby Football Union") and [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato_Rugby_Union "Waikato Rugby Union").

From 1996 to 1998 the Chiefs also represented [North Harbour](/wiki/North_Harbour_Rugby_Union "North Harbour Rugby Union") and [Northland](/wiki/Northland_Rugby_Union "Northland Rugby Union"), with Counties Manukau and Thames Valley falling under the [Auckland Blues](/wiki/Auckland_Blues "Auckland Blues") catchment. Had the Blues been allowed to represent the Auckland, North Harbour, Counties Manukau and Northland unions, they would have been able to field almost a full national team due to player contracting rules at the time. In an effort by the [NZRU](/wiki/New_Zealand_Rugby_Union "New Zealand Rugby Union") to make things more fair, the Chiefs were given North Harbour and Northland, while the Blues were given Counties Manukau and [Thames Valley](/wiki/Thames_Valley_Rugby_Football_Union "Thames Valley Rugby Football Union"). By 1999, clear regional dominance of national team players no longer existed, so North Harbour and Northland were 'returned' to the [Blues](/wiki/Blues_(Super_Rugby) "Blues (Super Rugby)") in exchange for Counties Manukau and Thames Valley.

Taranaki was originally part of the [Hurricanes](/wiki/Hurricanes_(rugby_union) "Hurricanes (rugby union)") from 1996, but switched to the Chiefs in 2013.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

East Coast was also originally part of the [Hurricanes](/wiki/Hurricanes_(rugby_union) "Hurricanes (rugby union)") from 1996, but as of February 2025 announced they had switched to the Chiefs.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiefs_(Super_Rugby)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ownership")]

The Chiefs are a wholly owned subsidiary of the NZRU. However, in an effort to bring more capital into the sport, the NZRU established a system of privatised operation in 2013. In 2014, it was announced that a new entity, 'Chiefs Rugby Club Limited Partnership', had been established, with the NZRU granting the newly formed company a seven-year licence, until the end of the 2020 season, to operate the club. Chiefs Rugby Club itself is 50% owned by the provincial unions within the Chiefs' catchment and 50% by a group of private investors.

## Development team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiefs_(Super_Rugby)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Development team")]

The Chiefs have fielded a development team in competitions such as the [Pacific Rugby Cup](/wiki/Pacific_Rugby_Cup "Pacific Rugby Cup") and in matches against other representative teams for several seasons. Known as the Chiefs Development XV or the Chiefs Taua, the squad is selected from the best emerging rugby talent in the Chiefs catchment area and is composed of Chiefs contracted players, wider training group members, under 20s, and selected club players.[[7]](#cite_note-dev-xv-7)[[8]](#cite_note-dev-xv-2-8)

#### Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chiefs_(Super_Rugby)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Notes")]

**[^1](#ref_One)** *Teams were awarded four points for a bye during the Super Rugby seasons from 2011 to 2013. Each team took two bye rounds each season. These additional 8 points are included in their season points tally.*  
**[^2](#ref_Two)** *All matches after Round 7 were cancelled. the season remained incomplete and no champion was awarded.*[[9]](#cite_note-9)  
**[^3](#ref_Three)** *Super Rugby Aotearoa was announced as a stand-in replacement competition for Super Rugby, between New Zealand Super Rugby sides. It was played as a round robin competition, with no finals. All teams played the other four teams twice, with the title awarded to the highest ranked team at the conclusion of the round robin fixtures.*[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)  
**[^4](#ref_Four)** *Super Rugby Aotearoa adopted the same format in 2021 as the inaugural tournament in 2020, with the addition of a final between the top two ranked teams at the conclusion of the round robin stage.*[[12]](#cite_note-12)  
**[^5](#ref_Five)** *Super Rugby Trans Tasman was a crossover competition between the teams involved in [Super Rugby Aotearoa](/wiki/2021_Super_Rugby_Aotearoa_season "2021 Super Rugby Aotearoa season") and [Super Rugby AU](/wiki/2021_Super_Rugby_AU_season "2021 Super Rugby AU season"). Each team from Super Rugby AU played each team from Super Rugby Aotearoa once, and vice versa. A final was played between the top two seeded teams at the conclusion of the round robin matches.*[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
